retrieved_docs.title: Iron -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Oral iron – Iron administered orally travels to the duodenum, where it is taken up into enterocyte cells along the brush border by the divalent metal-ion transporter 1 (DMT1). From there, it can either be incorporated into ferritin for storage or expelled into the serum by the ferroportin 1 transporter on the basolateral membrane. This transporter is bound to multicopper oxidases that oxidize ferrous iron (Fe2+) into ferric iron (Fe3+) that can bind to transferrin for transport to the bone marrow for hemoglobin synthesis or to the liver for storage. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Cellular Level -- Iron
retrieved_docs.content: Iron consumed from food can be present in two forms: heme and nonheme iron. 90% of dietary iron consists of nonheme iron, which is far less bioavailable than heme iron. Iron is absorbed in the small intestine in the duodenum. Given nonheme iron is often present in the form of ferric iron, it must be reduced to the ferrous form prior to enterocyte uptake with the ferric reductase enzyme DCYTB. The apical surface of the enterocyte contains divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1), which transports ferrous iron into the enterocyte. On the basolateral membrane of the enterocyte, ferroportin releases ferrous iron to hephaestin, which oxidizes ferrous iron to ferric iron so it can bind to the transporting protein transferrin in portal circulation. Ferroportin is the main regulatory point of entry for iron in the body. Iron is stored in the liver bound to ferritin, where it can be sequestered to bone marrow for erythropoiesis or red blood cell formation. Macrophages in the reticuloendothelial system of the liver, spleen, and bone marrow can ingest old red blood cells to recycle iron to be stored in the liver. There is no specific excretory system for iron. Iron loss can only occur secondary to the exfoliation of epithelial cells in the skin and gastrointestinal tract in addition to red blood cell loss from the gastrointestinal tract. [22] [42] [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Manganese Toxicity -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Transport of Mn3+ (oxidized form) is via transferrin in the bloodstream and transported across cell membranes via transferrin-receptor complexes. Transferrin receptors are located on cells of the BBB, neurons, and other cells of the central nervous system (CNS). [30] This ability of oxidized Mn to be carried via transferrin is one of the means that Fe deficiency contributes to manganism, given that transferrin levels are increased in the setting of iron deficiency. [31] Furthermore, deficiencies in Fe can lead to the up-regulation of Mn absorptive transporters in the intestines such as DMT-1, leading to Mn overload. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Iron Absorption -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Enterocyte DMT1 and Dcytb levels are upregulated in cases of iron deficiency anemia, and mutations in DMT1 have been shown to give rise to microcytic anemias and liver iron overload. [6] [7] Conditions that degrade the mucosa of the duodenum will decrease absorption of iron and include: Celiac disease Tropical sprue Crohn’s disease Duodenal cancer Duodenal ulcers Familial adenomatous polyposis

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Iron Absorption -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The absorption of most dietary iron occurs in the duodenum and proximal jejunum and depends heavily on the physical state of the iron atom. At physiological pH, iron exists in the oxidized, ferric (Fe3+) state. To be absorbed, iron must be in the ferrous (Fe2+) state or bound by a protein such as heme. The low pH of gastric acid in the proximal duodenum allows a ferric reductase enzyme, duodenal cytochrome B (Dcytb), on the brush border of the enterocytes to convert the insoluble ferric (Fe3+) to absorbable ferrous (Fe2+) ions. Gastric acid production plays a key role in plasma iron homeostasis. When proton-pump inhibiting drugs such as omeprazole are used, iron absorption is greatly reduced. Once ferric iron is reduced to ferrous iron in the intestinal lumen, a protein on the apical membrane of enterocytes called divalent metal cation transporter 1 (DMT1) transports iron across the apical membrane and into the cell. Levels of DMT1 and Dcytb are upregulated in the hypoxic environment of the intestinal mucosa by hypoxia-inducible factor-2 (HIF-2α).

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Iron Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The maintenance of iron homeostasis occurs via a balance of absorption and iron losses. Absorption in the small intestine is the primary regulating factor of iron content in the body. Iron is absorbed in divalent form by enterocytes via the divalent metal transporter (DMT1). Once absorbed, it is sequestered in ferritin if body stores are adequate. If iron levels are low, iron is bound to transferrin by ferroportin. Once loaded on to transferrin, iron is transported to cells for metabolism. Hepcidin is a peptide that inhibits ferroportin and decreases the absorption of iron into circulation. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Supplementation -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The role of iron supplementation is to replace those iron stores and to encourage erythropoiesis and oxygen transportation throughout the body. Iron transport occurs via the divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) across the cell membrane, where it is incorporated and stored as ferritin in the macrophage. This form then is converted to an absorbable Fe2+ ion, then sequestered by transferrin to various sites in the body, including the bone marrow for RBC synthesis. The iron combines with other components such as porphyrin and globin chains to form hemoglobin, which transports oxygen from the lung to other organs in the body. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Iron Absorption -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The duodenal pH-dependent process of iron absorption is inhibited or enhanced by certain dietary compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron-Binding Capacity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Foods contain iron in heme and non-heme forms. Bound elemental iron is released in the stomach through the action of hydrochloric acid. Ferric iron is enzymatically reduced to ferrous iron and is absorbed in the gut by the divalent metal ion transporter located on the apical surface of the intestinal epithelium. [17] Heme iron is absorbed directly through a heme transporter. Absorbed iron is stored with apoferritin within the enterocytes or absorbed into the blood through ferroportin. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Iron Absorption -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Despite its relative abundance in the environment and the relatively low daily iron requirements (10 mg ingested/1 mg absorbed) of humans, iron is often a growth-limiting nutrient in the human diet. Low intake of iron accounts for most anemia in developed countries and is responsible for nearly half of the anemias in non-industrialized nations. One reason for the lack of adequate iron absorption is that upon exposure to oxygen, iron forms highly insoluble oxides, which are unavailable for absorption in the human gastrointestinal tract. Human enterocytes contain apical membrane-bound enzymes whose activity can be regulated and which function to reduce insoluble ferric (Fe3+) to absorbable ferrous (Fe2+) ions.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Iron Absorption -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Once inside the enterocyte, iron can be stored as ferritin or transported through the basolateral membrane and into circulation bound to ferroportin. (Ferritin that is not bound to iron is called apoferritin, which has an intrinsic catalytic activity that oxidizes ferrous iron into ferric iron to be bound and stored as ferritin.)

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Dextran -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: The absorption of iron dextran after intramuscular (IM) administration may take up to 3 weeks. Reticulocyte count peaks 5 to 10 days after iron dextran administration, and hemoglobin increases in 2 to 4 weeks. [13] Distribution: Transferrin transports iron to the bone marrow for red blood cell production. The liver stores most of the excess iron in the form of ferritin or hemosiderin. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Dietary Iron -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Iron is absorbed in the small intestine, primarily in the duodenum and upper jejunum. Once absorbed, most binds to transferrin, a transport protein that delivers it to the bone marrow for erythropoiesis. The liver takes up non–transferrin-bound iron and stores it as ferritin. [4] This prevents significant amounts of iron from existing in its free form, which could damage proteins and cell membranes by free radical formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Manganese Toxicity -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Other known important proteins of Mn in the CNS include zinc transport proteins, the dopamine transporter (DAT), calcium channels, choline transporters, and citrate transporters. [20] DAT transporters are highly prevalent in the basal ganglia alongside the DMT-1 protein. DAT expression has been shown to be directly related to environmental exposure of Mn. [33] Mn has been shown to enter cells via store-operated calcium channels (SOCCs), which are present in the endothelial cells in the CNS, thus adding another route of Mn entry into the brain. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Iron Absorption -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: If hepcidin levels are low and ferroportin is not downregulated, ferrous iron (Fe2+) can be released from the enterocyte, where it is oxidized once again into ferric iron (Fe3+) for binding to transferrin, which is its carrier protein present in the plasma. Two copper-containing enzymes, ceruloplasmin in the plasma and hephaestin on the basolateral membrane of the enterocyte, catalyze the oxidation of and subsequent binding of ferrous iron to transferrin in the plasma. The principal role of transferrin is to chelate iron to be rendered soluble, prevent the formation of reactive oxygen species, and facilitate its transport into cells.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Dextran -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: After administering iron dextran, macrophages remove iron dextran from the bloodstream into the reticuloendothelial system (RES) via endocytosis. [6] [7] The RES separates the components of iron dextran. [8] Iron binds to transferrin, an iron-binding protein that transports iron to the bone marrow. [9] The iron-transferrin complex binds to transferrin receptor 1 (TFR1) expressed on erythroid progenitor cells, and iron dissociates from transferrin. [10] Erythroid progenitor cells use iron to produce heme, a component of hemoglobin. [11] In the presence of erythropoietin, erythroid progenitor cells mature into erythrocytes. [12] Hemoglobin is a component of the mature erythrocytes that transport oxygen in the body. Red blood cell count increases about 7 days after iron dextran infusion. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate are both members of the therapeutic class identified as iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations. This class comprises oral formulations that deliver trivalent iron to patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, thereby replenishing depleted iron stores and supporting hemoglobin synthesis. By belonging to the same category, ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate share a common mechanism of action and are used interchangeably as oral options for correcting iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2003
retrieved_docs.content: Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations are classified as members of the sodium ironedetate group, indicating that sodium ironedetate is a specific type of iron trivalent oral antianemic medication used to treat iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: and manganese is noted, suggesting formulation considerations. Collectively, these multivitamin‑iron and zinc products aim to improve hematopoiesis, correct simultaneous micronutrient deficiencies, enhance iron absorption (often facilitated by vitamin C and chelated iron forms), reduce gastrointestinal side effects, and provide comprehensive metabolic support for patients at risk of iron‑deficiency anemia, folate‑deficiency anemia, combined nutritional deficits, or those requiring bariatric supplementation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 129
retrieved_docs.content: non‑heme iron absorption, while extended‑release technologies improve tolerability. Collectively, the iron‑containing multivitamin preparations are designed to prevent or treat iron‑deficiency anemia, macrocytic anemia, and broader micronutrient deficiencies, thereby supporting hematopoiesis, metabolic function, and overall nutritional health. An isolated data‑record anomaly links gentamicin‑sulfát with manganese, but no established clinical interaction exists.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 29
retrieved_docs.content: therapies, while ferric maltol, ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate serve dual roles as nutritional iron supplements and, in some cases, industrial aids. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the multifaceted uses of these agents across therapeutic, nutritional and industrial domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 147
retrieved_docs.content: while iron (as ferrous fumarate, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, polysaccharide iron complex, iron sucrose, ferric pyrophosphate, iron ascorbate, ferro‑cysteine glycine, or ferrous amino‑acid chelate) addresses iron‑deficiency anemia. The chelated form (FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE) exemplifies the “Iron in combination with folic acid” sub‑category, enhancing iron absorption when paired with folic acid. Additional vitamins and minerals—vitamin B‑complex, vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin K, vitamin B12, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, and essential fatty acids—provide synergistic support for hematopoiesis, red‑cell formation, and overall metabolic health. Collectively, these iron‑containing multivitamin and antianemic products integrate iron, folic acid, and a spectrum of micronutrients to correct anemia, prevent macro‑ and micro‑nutrient deficiencies, and promote comprehensive patient nutritional status.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: provides multiple iron salts (ferrous fumarate, polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous cysteine glycinate) together with vitamin C derivatives (ascorbic acid, magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate) that facilitate iron uptake, and a spectrum of B‑vitamins and cofactors that support erythropoiesis and overall metabolic health. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how specific oral iron preparations—whether bivalent iron‑ascorbate salts or broader multivitamin‑iron combinations—are organized within therapeutic classes and formulated to maximize iron bioavailability while addressing concurrent micronutrient deficiencies commonly seen in patients with iron deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 930
retrieved_docs.content: both as active therapeutic agents and as formulation excipients in oral, topical, and specialized products aimed at supporting metabolic health, cardiovascular function, and nutritional adequacy, particularly in populations with increased micronutrient needs such as individuals with diabetes or iron‑deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE is a pharmaceutical preparation that combines iron with folic acid and is classified as a member of the iron‑in‑combination‑with‑folic‑acid group, reflecting its role in providing both elemental iron and folate for the treatment of iron deficiency and support of folate-dependent metabolic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: “Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations,” underscoring its role as a bioavailable divalent iron source used to treat or prevent iron‑deficiency anemia. All described formulations illustrate how iron antianemic preparations are integrated into multivitamin‑plus‑mineral products to provide comprehensive oral supplementation of iron, folic acid, B‑vitamins, vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin D, calcium, magnesium, zinc, essential fatty acids, probiotics and numerous co‑factors, thereby addressing multiple micronutrient deficiencies and their hematologic consequences in diverse patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: to enhance absorption. * **Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations** encompass ferric maltol and sodium iron‑edetate, both trivalent iron complexes employed to treat iron deficiency. * **Iron in combination with folic acid** is exemplified by ferrous amino‑acid chelate, a formulation that pairs iron with folic acid to support hematopoiesis. * **Iron in other combinations** includes a multivitamin capsule (FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMS) that contains ascorbic acid 40 mg, ferrous fumarate 62.5 mg, folic acid 1 mg, niacin 3 mg, and polysaccharide iron complex 62.5 mg. This product is classified within the “iron in other combinations” subclass, highlighting its broader nutrient profile beyond iron alone. The multivitamin capsule also demonstrates how iron‑containing products can be formulated with additional vitamins (ascorbic acid, niacin) and mineral complexes to improve therapeutic efficacy and patient compliance. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the structured taxonomy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 106
retrieved_docs.content: belongs to “Iron in combination with folic acid,” and iron ascorbate remains within “Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations.” Collectively, these relationships demonstrate a therapeutic strategy in which iron is frequently combined with folic acid and a wide array of vitamins, minerals, antioxidants, essential fatty acids, and probiotics to enhance iron absorption, correct iron‑deficiency and folate‑deficiency anemias, and provide comprehensive nutritional repletion for patients such as those undergoing bariatric surgery or those with multiple micronutrient deficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 101
retrieved_docs.content: and Iron,” “Vitamin B12 with C, Folic Acid and Iron,” and “FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMINS”—combine folic acid (typically 1 mg) with iron sources such as ferrous fumarate, polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous asparto‑glycinate or iron ascorbate, and often include vitamin B12, vitamin C, niacin, essential fatty acids, docusate or probiotics to enhance absorption. Specific formulations (e.g., ascorbic acid 40 mg / ferrous fumarate 62.5 mg / folic acid 1 mg / niacin 3 mg / polysaccharide iron complex 62.5 mg) exemplify this pattern. Chelated iron products such as “FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE” are also classified under “iron in combination with folic acid,” reinforcing the therapeutic strategy of pairing iron with folic acid. From a pharmacologic classification perspective, the “Multivitamin Drug Class” maps to “Multivitamin preparation,” and iron bivalent oral antianemic preparations (e.g., iron ascorbate, “askorbát železnatý”) are placed in the same therapeutic family as iron‑fortified

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1454
retrieved_docs.content: multivitamin‑iron products, clinicians aim to prevent or correct folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and address broader nutritional gaps that may accompany conditions such as bariatric surgery, chronic malabsorption, or increased physiological demand. The combined presence of other B‑vitamins, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin E, essential fatty acids, minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine), probiotics, and docusate further enhances the therapeutic scope of these formulations, promoting overall micronutrient repletion, gastrointestinal health, and patient well‑being.
